instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: Assembly: Human-readable representation of machine code.
Machine code: what a computer actually runs.
The “atoms” that make up a program.

runs.text: The “atoms” that make up a program.

text_blocks.text: CPUs CAN actually be fairly simple in concept.

runs.text: CPUs CAN actually be fairly simple in concept.

text_blocks.text: Each CPU chooses its own machine code (and therefore its own style of assembly language)

runs.text: Each CPU chooses its own machine code (and therefore its own style of assembly language)

text_blocks.text: We will compare that to x86 today!

runs.text: We will compare that to x86 today!

images.ai_description: A colorful, expression-filled emoji displaying surprise or stress, with wide eyes, an open mouth, and a sweat drop. Perfect for illustrating emotion in digital communication.

text_blocks.text: This command copies data fromone place to another.
Surprise! It’s actually shorthandfor a different set of instructions.

runs.text: for a different set of instructions.

text_blocks.text: The processor can be made simpler.

runs.text: The processor can be made simpler.

text_blocks.text: This command gets transformedinto a numerical representation.
The processor then interprets the binary representation.

runs.text: The processor then interprets the binary representation.

text_blocks.text: That’s essentially all a computer does!

runs.text: That’s essentially all a computer does!

text_blocks.text: CS 447 looks at this in much greater detail.

runs.text: CS 447 looks at this in much greater detail.

images.ai_description: This image shows an 8-pin integrated circuit chip, typically used in electronic devices for processing tasks. It's essential for understanding basic digital electronics.

text_blocks.text: Machine language instructions are the patterns of bits that a processor reads to know what to do

runs.text: that a processor reads to know what to do

text_blocks.text: Assembly language (or "asm") is a human-readable (mostly), textual representation of machine language.
Assembly is “fast”, so we should use it for everything!       			--- NO!!! ---

runs.text: Assembly is “fast”, so we should use it for everything!

text_blocks.text: No type-checking, no control structures, very few abstractions.	--- Fairly impractical for large things ---

runs.text: No type-checking, no control structures, very few abstractions.	---

text_blocks.text: So, large programs have to be rewritten (usually) to work on new things.

runs.text: So, large programs have to be rewritten (usually) to work on new things.

text_blocks.text: Critical paths and “boot” code in Kernels / Operating Systems

runs.text: Critical paths and “boot” code in Kernels / Operating Systems

text_blocks.text: Real-time programs (video games; tho increasingly less / abstracted away)

input.title: Architecture Sits at the Hardware Interface

text_blocks.text: Architecture Sits at the Hardware Interface

runs.text: Architecture Sits at the Hardware Interface

text_blocks.text: Legal “gray-area,” / “confusing-mess” but generally modification/reverse engineering is allowed. Kinda? (Section 1201, US Code 17 § 108, etc)

runs.text: Legal “gray-area,” / “confusing-mess” but generally modification/reverse engineering is allowed.

text_blocks.text: Removing copy protection in order to preserve/backup.

runs.text: Removing copy protection in order to preserve/backup.

text_blocks.text: Librarians and preservationists and “pirates” alike may all use/view/write assembly for this!

runs.text: ” alike may all use/view/write assembly for this!

text_blocks.text: I know someone that patched (the freely distributed) Lost Vikings so it would avoid copy protection and use a different sound configuration (so I could run it in a browser emulator)

runs.text: I know someone that patched (the freely distributed) Lost Vikings so it would avoid copy protection and use a different sound configuration (so I could run it in a browser emulator)

text_blocks.text: org  0x100           ; .com files always start 256 bytes into the segment

runs.text: ; .com files always start 256 bytes into the segment

text_blocks.text: mov  dx, msg         ; the address of or message in dx

runs.text: ; the address of or message in dx

text_blocks.text: mov  dx, fname       ; open file to patch

images.ai_description: Three cartoon Vikings with weapons and armor are depicted in a colorful, pixelated art style. Title "The Lost Vikings" is prominently displayed below.

text_blocks.text: Programs written in C, etc are generally translated into assembly.
You can look at the machine code of programs and get an assembly code listing.

runs.text: look at the machine code of programs and get an assembly code listing.

text_blocks.text: And step through the program one instruction at a time.

runs.text: And step through the program one instruction at a time.

text_blocks.text: When programs crash (sometimes programs you don’t have the code for) you can look at the assembly code and assess.

runs.text: When programs crash (sometimes programs you don’t have the code for) you can look at the assembly code and assess.

text_blocks.text: Programs exist to help you (gdb, IDA Pro, radare, etc)
We will apply this knowledge (using gdb) in a future assignment!
x86 really puts the… you know what… in Assembly

runs.text: x86 really puts the… you know what… in Assembly

text_blocks.text: An ISA is the interface that a CPU presents to the programmer.

runs.text: interface that a CPU presents to the programmer

text_blocks.text: When we say "architecture," this is what we mean.
What the CPU can do (add, subtract, call functions, etc.)
What registers it has (we'll get to those)
That is, the bit patterns used to encode instructions.

runs.text: That is, the bit patterns used to encode instructions.

text_blocks.text: …if there's any hardware at all (think of Java, etc: virtual/hypothetical ISAs)

runs.text: if there's any hardware at all (think of Java,

text_blocks.text: ISA designed to make it easy to:

runs.text: ISA designed to make it easy to:

text_blocks.text: write compilers that make machine code
ISA designed for humans to write asm.
Lots of instructions and ways to use them

runs.text: of instructions and ways to use them

text_blocks.text: Complex (multi-step) instructions to shortenand simplify programs.
"check the bounds of an array access“

runs.text: "check the bounds of an array access“

text_blocks.text: Without these, you’d just write your programs to use the simpler instructions to build the complex behavior itself.

runs.text: Without these, you’d just write your programs to use the simpler instructions to build the complex behavior itself.

text_blocks.text: CISC: Complex Instruction Set Computer (does a whole lot)

runs.text: : Complex Instruction Set Computer (does a whole lot)

text_blocks.text: RISC: Reduced Instruction Set Computer (does enough)
Both: Equivalent!! (RISC programs might be longer)
“Hackers” (1995) – Of course, they are talking about a Pentium x86 chip… which thanks to its backwards compatibility, is CISC. Oh well!

runs.text: “Hackers” (1995) – Of course, they are talking about a Pentium x86 chip… which thanks to its backwards compatibility, is

text_blocks.text: Then again… x86 is so complex, modern designs translate the CISC instructions into RISC microcode on the fly… so it’s RISC?? It can get complicated.

runs.text: Then again… x86 is so complex, modern designs translate the CISC instructions into RISC microcode on the fly… so it’s RISC?? It can get complicated.

text_blocks.text: Each version can run most programs from the previous version.
You can (mostly) run programs written in ‘78 on your brand new x86 CPU!

runs.text: You can (mostly) run programs written in ‘78 on your brand new x86 CPU!

text_blocks.text: We won’t exhaustively go over it.

runs.text: We won’t exhaustively go over it.

text_blocks.text: There are, however, many very common idiomsand instructions.

runs.text: There are, however, many very common idioms

text_blocks.text: And we will focus on READING x86, not writing it.

runs.text: And we will focus on READING x86, not writing it.

images.ai_description: Blue "Intel Inside" logo featuring stylized text within an oval. Represents Intel's branding and presence as a leading microprocessor manufacturer in the technology industry.

text_blocks.text: Like MIPS, there are a set of general-purpose registers.

runs.text: Like MIPS, there are a set of general-purpose registers.

text_blocks.text: There are 16; 64-bits in size and hold integer values in binary form.

runs.text: There are 16; 64-bits in size and hold integer values in binary form.

text_blocks.text: Unlike MIPS, you can refer to parts of each register.

runs.text: Unlike MIPS, you can refer to parts of each register.

input.title: 32-bit OLD Registers – 32 bits wide
input.notes:
Only 6 registers that we can do whatever we want with
Remember how I said we’ve been upgrading x86 since 1978, when it was 16-bit?
Originally just %ax, …
Could even reference single bytes (high, low)
There used to be more conventions about how they were used
Just to make it easier when people were writing assembly by hand
Now irrelevant

text_blocks.text: There are also registers that you cannot directly interact with.

runs.text: There are also registers that you cannot directly interact with.

text_blocks.text: Like MIPS, x86 has a program counter ( %rip )
Also like MIPS, it cannot be read directly.

runs.text: Also like MIPS, it cannot be read directly.

text_blocks.text: There is also a FLAGS status register, which has information about the CPU state after an instruction is completed.

runs.text: status register, which has information about the CPU state after an instruction is completed.

text_blocks.text: Stuff like a carry flag (CF) that denotes if an addition has a final carry.

runs.text: Stuff like a carry flag (CF) that denotes if an addition has a final carry.

text_blocks.text: Overflow detection (OF) denoting if an operation overflowed.

runs.text: Overflow detection (OF) denoting if an operation overflowed.

text_blocks.text: And some extra registers for vector math, floating point math, and for OS usage we won’t go over.

runs.text: And some extra registers for vector math, floating point math, and for OS usage we won’t go over.

text_blocks.text: In MIPS, you had R-type, I-type and J-type instructions.

runs.text: In MIPS, you had R-type, I-type and J-type instructions.

text_blocks.text: In x86 (CISC) you generally can have any instruction refer to data anywhere it is:

runs.text: In x86 (CISC) you generally can have any instruction refer to data anywhere it is:

text_blocks.text: Cannot refer to memory twice! (not possible: mov (ptr), (ptr2) )

runs.text: Cannot refer to memory twice! (not possible:

text_blocks.text: add  t0, zero, t1	   # t0 = t1
In MIPS, you would carefully craft the set of instructions necessary to interface with an array. (RISC)

runs.text: In MIPS, you would carefully craft the set of instructions necessary to interface with an array. (RISC)

text_blocks.text: In x86, you can do a lot with just a single instruction. (CISC)

runs.text: In x86, you can do a lot with just a single instruction. (CISC)

text_blocks.text: (Rb, Ri, S): Base + (Index * Scalar) where Scalar must be 1, 2, 4 or 8

runs.text: Scalar) where Scalar must be 1, 2, 4 or 8

text_blocks.text: The fields are all optional; i.e., (,Ri, S) does just Index * Scalar

runs.text: The fields are all optional; i.e.,

text_blocks.text: la  t0, arr     # t0 = address to arr
add t0, t0, t1  # t0 = t0 + t1
LEA simply computes address (no memory access)

runs.text: LEA simply computes address (no memory access)

text_blocks.text: When we say you can do a lot with just a single instruction, we mean it!

runs.text: When we say you can do a lot with just a single instruction, we mean it!

text_blocks.text: (Rb, Ri, S): Base + (Index * Scalar) where Scalar must be 1, 2, 4 or 8

runs.text: Scalar) where Scalar must be 1, 2, 4 or 8

text_blocks.text: LEA simply computes address… it’s just very specific math.

runs.text: LEA simply computes address… it’s just very specific math.

text_blocks.text: In MIPS, you sometimes had instructions varying on bitsize.

runs.text: In MIPS, you sometimes had instructions varying on

text_blocks.text: In x86 (CISC) you can operate on any part of a register.

runs.text: In x86 (CISC) you can operate on any part of a register.

text_blocks.text: The assembler can assume usually, but explicit names also work:

runs.text: The assembler can assume usually, but explicit names also work:

text_blocks.text: Ugh. In x86 a “word” here is 16-bits
x86 and MIPS have, essentially, the same mathematical instructions.

runs.text: x86 and MIPS have, essentially, the same mathematical instructions.

text_blocks.text: add  t0, t0, t1  # t0 += t1
sub  t0, t0, t1  # t0 -= t1
sra  t0, t0, t1  # t0 >>= t1
srl  t0, t0, t1  # t0 >>= t1
sll  t0, t0, t1  # t0 <<= t1
xor  t0, t0, t1  # t0 ^= t1

input.title: Brought to you by the letters: C ABI

text_blocks.text: Brought to you by the letters: C ABI

runs.text: Brought to you by the letters: C ABI

text_blocks.text: The C Application Binary Interface (ABI) are assembly conventions
Like MIPS, certain registers are typically used for returns values, args, etc

runs.text: Like MIPS, certain registers are typically used for returns values,

text_blocks.text: It is not defined by the language, but rather the OS.

runs.text: It is not defined by the language, but rather the OS.

text_blocks.text: Windows and Linux (UNIX/System V) have a different C ABI 

runs.text: Windows and Linux (UNIX/System V) have a different C ABI

text_blocks.text: In our x86-64 Linux C ABI, registers are used to pass arguments:

runs.text: In our x86-64 Linux C ABI, registers are used to pass arguments:

text_blocks.text: Remaining arguments go on the stack.

runs.text: Remaining arguments go on the stack.

text_blocks.text: Callee must preserve %rbp, %rbx, %r12, %r13, %r14, %r15 (Like MIPS s0 – s7)
Return value: %rax (overflows into %rdx for 128-bits) (MIPS v0 – v1)
Lots of other small things not worth going over.

runs.text: Lots of other small things not worth going over.
